text: Large Language Model (LLM)-empowered multi-agent systems extend the cognitive boundaries of individual agents through disciplined collaboration and interaction, while constructing these systems often requires labor-intensive manual designs.

Despite the availability of methods to automate the design of agentic workflows, they typically seek to identify a static, complex, one-size-fits-all system, which, however, fails to dynamically allocate inference resources based on the difficulty and domain of each query.

To address this challenge, we shift away from the pursuit of a monolithic agentic system, instead optimizing the agentic supernet , a probabilistic and continuous distribution of agentic architectures.
We introduce , an automated framework that samples query-dependent agentic systems from the supernet, delivering high-quality solutions and tailored resource allocation ( e.g.
, LLM calls, tool calls, token cost).

Comprehensive evaluation across six benchmarks demonstrates that (I) requires only $6 45\%$ of the inference costs of existing handcrafted or automated multi-agent systems, (II) surpasses them by $0.54\% 16.89\%$, and (III) enjoys superior cross-dataset and cross-LLM-backbone transferability.
The code is available at .

-0.5em

Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents autogpt,babyagi,agentgpt have made remarkable strides in a spectrum of domains, such as question answering zhu2024autotqa , data analysis hong2024datainterpreter,li2024autokaggle , code generation reflexion , web navigation deng2024mind2web , and data synthesis butt2024benchagents , by equipping LLMs with high-level features, including persona multi-persona,chen2024persona , tools shen2024hugginggpt,autogpt , planning ACL2024_AUTOACT-Self-Planning,wu2024Graph4planning,he2023lego , and memory zhong2024memorybank,hatalis2023memorymatter,packer2023memgpt .

Building upon the success of single agents, researchers have demonstrated that combining multiple agents, either cooperatively zhuge2024gptswarm or competitively zhao2023competeai , can surpass the cognitive and intellectual capabilities of individuals arXiv2023_MultiAgent-Debate,arXiv2023_MultiAgent-Debate_2,multi-persona,blender,autogen,zhang2024cut , showcasing the collective intelligence in a society of LLM-agents piatti2024societyofagents .

Early multi-agent systems, such as CAMEL NeurIPS2023camel , AutoGen autogen , and MetaGPT meta-gpt , while delivering specialized capacity, often heavily rely on manual configurations, including prompt engineering, agent profiling, and inter-agent communication pipelines qian2024scaling .
This dependency significantly limits the rapid adaptation of multi-agent systems to diverse domains and application scenarios tang2023verifai,zhang2024aflow .
More recently, the research community has shifted toward automating multi-agent system design.

For instance, DsPy khattab2023dspy and EvoPrompting guo2023evoprompt automate prompt optimization, GPTSwarm zhuge2024gptswarm and G-Designer zhang2024gdesigner optimize inter-agent communication, and EvoAgent yuan2024evoagent and AutoAgents chen2023autoagents self-evolve agent profiling.
Nevertheless, they typically focus on automating specific aspects of the system.
Subsequently, ADAS hu2024adas , AgentSqure shang2024agentsquare , and AFlow zhang2024aflow broaden the design search space.
These state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods optimize a single, complex (multi-)agent workflow for a given dataset via different search paradigms, e.g.
, heuristic search hu2024adas , Monte Carlo tree search zhang2024aflow , and evolution shang2024agentsquare , surpassing the performance of manually designed systems.

[t]

figures/intro.pdf
-2em
( Left ) The building blocks of ; ( Right ) When confronting different queries, the agentic supernet adaptively samples tailored multi-agent architecture in a query-dependent manner.

-0.5em

Although the paradigm of searching for a one-size-fits-all multi-agent system appears sufficient to optimize performance-related metrics such as accuracy and pass@k , its performance is largely constrained on resource-related metrics, such as token cost, LLM calls, and inference latency ( Dilemma 1 ).
Specifically, contemporary methods tend to optimize for a complex and resource-intensive agentic system, often involving dozens of LLM API calls and external tool usage arXiv2023_Dynamic-LLM-Agent .

However, this is far from an optimal solution: for example, in mathematical benchmarks hendrycksmath2021 , Ph.D.-level abstract algebra may indeed require complicated, token-heavy systems, while simple elementary-level arithmetic works well with a single zero-shot I/O.

This paradigm becomes even more problematic when applied to benchmarks across multiple task domains ( Dilemma 2 ): for instance, in the GAIA benchmark mialon2023gaiabenchmark , there is no single system that is optimal for both file reading and web searching tasks, leaving practitioners with no alternative but to split the benchmark and optimize separately zhuge2024gptswarm .
These dilemmas unveil that, the paradigm of automatically optimizing a single multi-agent architecture fails to meet the dynamic and evolving demands of agentic deployment.

To address the above challenges, we propose Multi-agent Architecture Search ( ) , which, instead of searching for a plausible (possibly non-existent) optimal solution, generates a distribution of multi-agent systems.
Technically, we model the optimization of on the agentic supernet , a probabilistic, continuous agentic architecture distribution that encompasses a vast number of possible multi-agent candidates.
The agentic supernet can be seen as a cascaded multi-layer workflow, including 182 multiple agentic operators ( e.g.
, CoT cot , Multi-agent Debate arXiv2023_MultiAgent-Debate , ReAct yao2023react ), as well as 183 the parameterized probability distributions of operators across layers.
During training, leverages a controller network to sample multi-agent architectures conditioned on input queries.

The distribution parameters and operators are jointly updated based on environmental feedback, with the former's gradients approximated via Monte Carlo sampling and the latter's via textual gradient estimation.

During inference, for different queries, samples a suitable multi-agent system delivering satisfactory resolution and appropriate inference resources, thereby achieving task-customized collective intelligence.

We conduct comprehensive evaluations on seven widely adopted benchmarks, covering diverse use cases in code generation (HumanEval, MBPP), mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH, SVAMP), and diverse tool usage (GAIA).

Empirical results demonstrate that is 182 high-performing , surpassing existing handcrafted or automated multi-agent systems by $0.54\% 16.89\%$; 183 token-economical , outperforming the SOTA baseline AFlow on the MATH benchmark with $15\%$ of the training cost and $25\%$ of the inference cost; 184 transferable across datasets and LLM-backbones; 185 inductive , demonstrating strong generalizability to unseen agentic operators.

Briefly put, our key contributions are summarized as follows:
-1.8em
[leftmargin=*,itemsep=-0.2em]
Paradigm Reformulation : We introduce the concept of agentic supernet , a probabilistic, continuous agentic architecture distribution, which transforms the paradigm of optimizing a single optimal multi-agent system into optimizing the distribution of multiple architectures.

Practical Solution : We propose , an agentic supernet-based framework that automatically evolves powerful multi-agent systems and adaptively allocates high-performing and resource-efficient solutions for user queries with varied difficulty, domain and features.

Experimental Evaluation : Extensive evaluations on six benchmarks demonstrate that our framework discovers novel agentic systems with $0.54\% 16.89\%$ higher performance, significantly lower training/inference costs, transferability across benchmarks and LLMs, and superior inductive capacity.

-1em

[t]

-0.5em

-0.3em
LLM-Agents and Agentic Systems.

Building on the success of single agents shen2024hugginggpt,zhu2024knowagent,zhong2024memorybank , studies have shown that grouping multiple LLM-based agents into multi-agent systems (MAS) can substantially enhance individual model capabilities FCS2024_Survey-Agent , as demonstrated in early attempts such as AutoGen autogen , LLM-Debate arXiv2023_MultiAgent-Debate , and AgentVerse chen2023agentverse .
However, they heavily relied on manually crafted designs, which constrained the adaptability and flexibility of agents in addressing unforeseen challenges he2023lego,chen2023agentverse .
As a result, automated agentic system design has gained increasing attention in the academic community.

-0.6em
Automating Agentic Systems.

Efforts to automate the design of agent-based systems can be broadly classified into the following categories: (I) Prompt Optimization , such as PromptBreeder fernando2023promptbreeder , DsPy khattab2023dspy , and EvoPrompt guo2023evoprompt ; (II) Inter-agent Communication , which focuses on orchestrating interactions between agents, including GPTSwarm zhuge2024gptswarm , DyLAN arXiv2023_Dynamic-LLM-Agent , EvoMAC hu2024evomac , AgentPrune zhang2024cut and G-Designer zhang2024gdesigner ; and (III) Agent Profiling , represented by AgentVerse chen2023agentverse , EvoAgent yuan2024evoagent , and AutoAgents chen2023autoagents .

Further, ADAS hu2024adas and AgentSquare shang2024agentsquare provide more comprehensive automation for single-agent design, while AFlow zhang2024aflow achieves multi-agent workflow automation using Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS).

However, these high-performing methods still follow the paradigm of searching for a single final system, whereas searches for distribution of architectures with lower average inference costs (LLM calls, token cost, etc.

-0.6em
AutoML.
Automating the design of agentic systems is an emerging topic, yet the history of AutoML he2021automl provides clear precedents.
Notably, the progression of agentic automation mirrors that of neural architecture search (NAS) ren2021comprehensivenas .

Core NAS techniques, such as reinforcement learning zoph2016rl-nas , evolutionary algorithms liu2021evo-nas , Bayesian optimization (BO) white2021bananas , and MCTS wang2021mcts-nas , have inspired analogous approaches in agentic automation, from policy gradient in zhuge2024gptswarm to evolutionary search in yuan2024evoagent , BO in shang2024agentsquare , and MCTS in zhang2024aflow .
In NAS, however, these black-box methods were eventually eclipsed by efficient supernet training white2023nas1k , culminating in seminal works like DARTS liu2018darts and SNAS xie2018snas .
Inspired by this, we introduce the first MAS searching framework leveraging an agentic supernet , posing new paradigms and challenges for agentic automation.

-0.4em

-0.4em
fig:framework illustrates the overall workflow of our method.
takes diverse and varying difficulty queries as input and leverages a controller to sample a subnetwork from the agentic supernet for each query, corresponding to a customized multi-agent system.
After the sampled system executes the query, receives environment feedback and jointly optimizes the supernet’s parameterized distribution and agentic operators.

In the following sections, sec:prelim formally defines the search space and optimization objective of , sec:arch-sample details how the controller query-dependently samples multi-agent structures, and sec:gradient-update details the optimization of .

-0.4em

-0.4em
Search Space.

We first define the basic unit of 's search space, namely the agentic operator as follows:
[ Agentic Operator ]

An agentic operator $ O $ is a composite LLM-agent invocation process that involves multiple LLM calls and tool usage:

O = \ \ M _i\ _ i=1 ^m, P , \ T _i\ _ i=1 ^n\ ,

M _i M , P P , T _i T ,

where $ M $ and $ M $ correspond to LLM backbones and the set of available LLMs, respectively.
Similarly, $ P $ and $ T $ represent prompts and tools.
$m$ and $n$ denote the number of LLM-agents and tools invoked in the operator, respectively.

-0.3em
Most existing single/multi-agent workflows can be viewed as agentic operators: CoT cot can be considered one with $m=1$ and $n=0$, denoted as $ O _ CoT $; Self-RAG asai2023self-rag similarly involves $m=1$ agent allocation, but is equipped with $n=1$ retrieval engine, denoted as $ O _ SRAG $; Multi-agent debate involves multiple LLM-agent, multi-turn calls, denoted as $ O _ Debate $.
The feasible set of agentic operators is denoted as $ O $, and we discuss the initialization of $ O $ in sec:exp-setup,app:operator .

We define a multi-agent system as:

G = \ V , E \ ,\; V O ,\; E V V ,

where $ V $ is the set of selected operators in $ G $ and $ E $ denotes their connectivity.
$ G $ is constrained as a direct acyclic graph (DAG).

Finally, we define the agentic supernet:

[ Agentic Supernet ]

The agentic supernet is denoted as \( A = \ , O \ =\ \ _ ( O )\ _ O O \ _ =1 ^L\), where:

_ ( O ) &= p( O A _ 1: -1 ), \;\; O O ,

A _ 1: -1 &= \ \ _ k ( O )\ _ O O \ _ k=1 ^ -1 ,

where \( _ ( O )\) represents the probability of operator \( O \) present at layer \( \), conditioned on the preceding layers \( A _ 1: -1 \).

The supernet induces a joint distribution over all possible multi-layer operator configurations:

p( G ) = _ =1 ^L _ O O _ ( O )^ I _ O V _ ,

where \( I _ O V _ \) is the indicator function for the inclusion of \( O \) in the set of active operators \( V _ \) at layer \( \).

-0.6em
Problem Formulation.

Given a benchmark $ D $ comprising multiple queries $q$ and their corresponding oracle answers/solutions $a$, the objective of is not to identify a single optimal agentic system like previous practices zhang2024aflow,zhuge2024gptswarm , but to optimize a conditional probability distribution as follows:

P ( G | q) \; E _ (q,a) D ,
G P ( G | q) ,\; s.t.

\; G A

where $ P ( G | q)$ is a distribution that generates query-dependent agentic architectures.
$U( )$ and $C( )$ represent the utiulity/performance and cost of $ G $ for query $q$, respectively, and $ $ is a trade-off parameter.

-0.5em

-0.5em
The core of lies in tailoring a customized multi-agent system for each user query, which may vary in difficulty and domain, to deliver a satisfactory solution:

p(a | q, , O ) = e(a | G ) \; Q _ ( G | q, , O ) \, d G ,

where $ Q _ $ represents the controller network , which takes the query $q$, the parameterized distribution $ $, and the available operators $ O $, and outputs the sampled agentic architecture $ G $.
$ Q _ $ is parameterized by $ $, and $e( | )$ denotes producing solution via executing $ G $.

we implement $ Q _ $ as follows:

Q _ ( G | q, , O ) = _ =1 ^L _ ( V _ | q, \ V _h\ _ h=1 ^ -1 ),

where $ V _h$ denotes the selected operators at layer $h$.
The selection of $ V _ $ is conditionally dependent on the query \( q \) and the operators from the previous layers.
However, not all queries require execution across \( L \) layers.
As discussed in sec:intro , many questions can be resolved with a simple zero-shot I/O zhang2024gdesigner , rendering \( L \) layers unnecessarily redundant.
To address this, we introduce an early-exit operator , denoted as \( O _ exit \).

During sampling, if \( O _ exit \) is encountered, the process exits early:

Q _ ( G | q, , O ) =& _ =1 ^ L

&+ I _ O _ exit V _ ( - _ exit ),

where \( _ exit \) denotes the layer at which \( O _ exit \) appears, and \( ( )\) is the Kronecker delta function.

We implement the sampling process $ _ $ with a Mixture-of-Expert (MoE)-style network shazeer2017outrageously,huang2024harder :

_ :\;q V _ ,\; V _ =\ O _ 1 , O _ 2 , , O _ t \ ,

t = k \ 1, ,| O |\ _ j<k S ^ _ k >thres,

where \( S ^ = sort ( S , desc )\), and \( S R ^ | O | = [S_1, , S_ | O | ]\) represents the activation scores of all feasible operators w.r.t.
Note that \(thres\) is a threshold value that governs operator activation.
Operators are activated sequentially, starting from the one with the highest score, and the process continues until the cumulative score exceeds \(thres\).
This ensures that the number of selected operators per layer is query-dependent, allowing to dynamically allocate resources based on task complexity.

\( S \) is given by: $
S_i = FFN ( v (q) \| _ O V _1 v ( O ) \| \| _ O V _ - 1 v ( O )),$
where \( v ( )\) denotes the embedding function using lightweight models like MiniLM wang2020minilm and SentenceBert reimers2019sentence , and \(\|\) represents concatenation.
The detailed implementation of $ v ( )$ is placed in app:embedding .
Upon completing the sequential sampling procedure in , a task-specific multi-agent system \( G \) is generated and executed to produce the answer \( a \).
In the next section, we elucidate the process of updating the agentic supernet based on environmental feedback.

-0.5em

-0.5em

We present the optimization objective of as follows:

, O \; E _ (q,a) D , G Q _

where \(C( )\) evaluates the cost of multi-agent systems, represented by token cost, and \( \) is the trade-off parameter.
The term \(p(a | q, , O )\) in eq:objective corresponds to eq:core , where the calculation of \(e(a | G )\) often involves external tools or API-based LLM calls, rendering it non-differentiable.

Therefore, we employ an empirical Bayes Monte Carlo procedure carlin2000empirical,yan2021fpnas to estimate the gradient w.r.t the distribution \( \):

_ L 1 K \!\!\!

_ k=1 ^K ,

m_k= p(a | q, G _k) _i p(a | q, G _i) - C( G _k;q) _i C( G _i;q) ,

where \(m_k\) denotes the cost-aware importance weights of the agentic architecture.
Intuitively, the distribution \( \) is updated to favor multi-agent systems that generate high-quality solutions with minimal token cost.

-1.em

However, the gradient w.r.t operators $ _ O L $ cannot be computed similarly.
As shown in eq:operator , operators include black-box tool usage and natural language prompts, making numerical gradient updates infeasible.

To address this, we utilize agent-based textual gradient chatllm-network,arXiv2023_Dynamic-LLM-Agent,hu2024evomac,zhou2024symbolic-evolve to approximate the backpropagation for agentic operators, as visualized in fig:gradient and formalized as follows:

_ O L = T _ P T _ T T _ N , T _x T , x \ P , T ,N\

where $ T _ P , T _ T , T _ N $ represent agent-generated gradient analyses in textual format, corresponding to updates of the prompt, model temperature, and operator node structure (such as merging, splitting, altering, etc.
), respectively.
See prompts in app:text-gradient .
In this way, the core components of the agentic supernet, namely the agentic operators and their connectivity, are jointly updated, enabling the fully automated evolution of multi-agent systems.
We summarize the notations in tab:notations , and the algorithm in alg:algo .

We evaluate on six public benchmarks covering three domains: (1) math reasoning , GSM8K gsm8k , MATH hendrycksmath2021 , and MultiArith ; (2) code generation , HumanEval human-eval and MBPP austin2021mbpp ); and (3) tool use , GAIA mialon2023gaiabenchmark .
For the MATH benchmark, we follow in selecting 617 problems from four typical problem types (Combinatorics \& Probability, Number Theory, Pre-algebra, Pre-calculus).
The dataset statistics are in app:dataset .

-0.8em
Baselines.

We compare with three series of agentic baselines: (1) single agent execution methods , including CoT , ComplexCoT , Self-Consistency ; (2) hand-craft multi-agent systems , including MultiPersona multi-persona , LLM-Debate arXiv2023_MultiAgent-Debate , LLM-Blender , DyLAN arXiv2023_Dynamic-LLM-Agent , AgentVerse chen2023agentverse and MacNet qian2024scaling ; (3) (partially or fully) autonomous multi-agent systems , including GPTSwarm zhuge2024gptswarm , AutoAgents chen2023autoagents , ADAS hu2024adas , AgentSquare shang2024agentsquare and AFlow zhang2024aflow .
More details on baseline setups are provided in app:baselines .

-0.8em
Implementation details.
We leverage both close-source LLM ( gpt-4o-mini-0718 OpenAI-gpt4o ) and open-source LLM ( Qwen-2.5-72b-instruct and llama-3.1-70b ).
All models are accessed via APIs with the temperature set to $1$.
We set the number of layers as $L=4$, the cost penalty coefficient $ $ as $ \ 1e-3,5e-3,1e-2\ $, and the sampling times $K=4$.
$thres=0.3$ for eq:layer-l .

182 achieves optimal performance across all task domains.
The multi-agent system optimized by outperforms manually designed methods by an average of $3.90 6.40\%$ and existing automated methods by $2.07 8.26\%$.
Overall, as for mathematical reasoning and code generation, achieves an average best score of $83.59\%$, demonstrating its versatility and superiority.
tab:rq1_gaia shows a comparison of with automated systems and three additional baselines, including AutoGPT autogpt , TapeAgent bahdanau2024tapeagents , and Sibyl wang2024sibyl on the GAIA benchmark.
GAIA encompasses tasks from various domains such as web browsing, file reading, and multimodal understanding, making it challenging to pursue a single optimal multi-agent system for all tasks.
Thus, the modest improvements of AFlow and ADAS over vanilla LLMs (only $3.35\% $ and $2.04\% $ on average) are understandable.
In contrast, can adaptively sample customized agentic systems for different domains, achieving $18.38\%$ and $17.61\%$ improvements on Level 1 and 2 tasks, respectively.

-0.3em

-0.4em
To answer RQ2, we demonstrate that is both training/inference cost-efficient from the following three dimensions: (1) token cost, (2) API cost, and (3) wall-clock time, as shown in tab:efficiency and fig:rq2-cost .

We observe:

Obs.
183 's optimization is resource-friendly.
As shown in fig:rq2-cost (Training Tokens) , among the various optimization-oriented agentic workflows, achieves the highest accuracy with the least training token consumption.
While AFlow's accuracy is comparable to that of , its training cost reaches $22.50\$$, which is $6.8 $ that of (merely $3.38\$$).
Additionally, existing agentic automation pipelines are relatively time-consuming, with DyLAN taking $508$ minutes and GPTSwarm taking $129$ minutes.
In contrast, the optimization wall-clock time of requires only $53$ minutes.
Obs.
184 Agentic supernet enjoys superior token economy during inference.
As shown in fig:rq2-cost (Inference API Cost) , achieves the highest accuracy with an API cost of $0.42\$$, demonstrating its high performance and token economy.
Although AgentSquare's API cost is slightly lower than 's, this is due to its limitation to a single-agent search, which severely restricts its performance (resulting in a $4\%$ drop compared to ).
tab:efficiency further highlights that has the lowest prompt/completion token consumption, the lowest API cost, and the shortest wall-clock time during inference.
These advantages can be attributed to the agentic supernet's ability to dynamically allocate resources based on the difficulty of the query.

-0.4em

-0.4em
In this section, we explore and visualize the intrinsic mechanisms of the agentic supernet.

fig:probability showcases the probability distributions of the agentic supernet when faced with different queries, and fig:case presents the multi-agent systems designed by for queries from the MATH, GAIA, and HumanEval benchmarks.

We have:

Obs.
185 learns to query-aware early exit from the reasoning process.

As shown in fig:probability , when faced with the easy queries (a) and (b), exits multi-agent architecture sampling at the second layer with probabilities of $0.37$ and $0.47$, respectively, selecting the early-exit operator.

Notably, query (b) chose two agentic operators at the first layer: direct I/O and ReAct, demonstrating 's ability to dynamically allocate different operators at each layer (corresponding to eq:layer-l ).
For the more challenging queries (c) and (d), sampled additional layers, further proving its ability to customize the multi-agent system based on query awareness.
This is also visualized by fig:dynamic , in which the probability of $ O _ exit $ becomes increasingly high with the supernet depth increases.

-0.9em

-0.6em

-0.4em
Sensitivity Analysis We analyze the sensitivity of to three core parameters: the number of layers in the agentic supernet $L$, the cost penalty coefficient $ $ in eq:objective , and the sampling count $K$ in eq:loss-pi .
The results are presented in fig:sensi .
For the parameter $L$ , we observe a significant performance improvement as $L$ increases from 2 to 4 ($89.5\% 92.8\%$).
However, further increases yield only marginal performance gains while incurring higher per-query inference costs.
Considering both performance and cost, we select $L = 4$.
For the parameter $ $ , we find that larger values lead to favor more cost-efficient solutions, albeit with some performance degradation.
For the parameter $K$ , we note that performance is suboptimal with highest variance when $K = 2$.
Increasing $K$ to 4 effectively achieves a satisfactory low-variance estimation.

-0.9em
Ablation Study We perform an ablation study on three key components of : (1) w/o $ _ O L $ , removing the textual gradient in eq:loss-operator ; (2) w/o $ O _ exit $ , removing the early-exit operator in eq:early-exit ; and (3) w/o $C( )$ , eliminating the cost constraint in eq:objective .
We observe from tab:ablation that removing the textual gradient causes the largest performance drop, as it disables 's self-evolving capability.
Removing $ O _ exit $ and $C( )$ results in little impact on performance, but it weakens 's query-dependent nature and unnecessarily increases the inference cost.

[!htpb]
-1em
Ablation study of .
We evaluate whether the agentic supernet of is (1) model-agnostic and (2) generalizable across datasets , with results presented in tab:cross-llm,tab:cross-dataset .
As shown, the agentic supernet optimized by transfers well to models such as Qwen-2.5-70b , with $4.98\% 5.50\% $ in performance, while also demonstrating strong cross-dataset generalization.

-0.4em
Inductive Analysis.
To evaluate whether possesses inductive capabilities, i.e.
, the ability to generalize to unseen agentic operators, we select the Debate operator as a holdout.
We then compare the operator distribution of during inference with and without Debate in fig:dynamic,fig:dynamic2 .
The results demonstrate that can still reasonably activate and utilize the unseen operator at an appropriate proportion.

-0.4em

-0.4em
In this paper, we for the first time shift the paradigm of automated multi-agent system design from seeking a (possibly non-existent) single optimal system to optimizing a probabilistic, continuous distribution of agentic architectures, termed the agentic supernet .

Building on this concept, we propose , which dynamically samples multi-agent systems that deliver satisfactory performance and token efficiency for user queries across different domains and varying levels of difficulty.
We believe that paves the way toward fully automated, self-organizing, and self-evolving collective intelligence.
This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.
92270114), and the Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Major Project.
Ethical Considerations.
We believe that our proposed framework raises no ethical concerns regarding its motivation, design, implementation, or data usage.

The method is designed to advance the automation of multi-agent systems in a principled and resource-efficient manner, ensuring responsible development while adhering to ethical guidelines in AI research.

-0.5em
Societal Implications.
introduces a new paradigm in multi-agent system design by replacing static, one-size-fits-all architectures with a dynamic and adaptive agentic supernet.
This approach enables fine-grained resource allocation tailored to query difficulty and domain, significantly improving efficiency while maintaining high-quality outputs.

By reducing inference costs and enhancing the flexibility of multi-agent workflows, has the potential to democratize access to intelligent automation across diverse applications, including education, research, and industry.

Notation & Definition

1.pt
$ O = \ \ M _i\ _ i=1 ^m, P , \ T _i\ _ i=1 ^n\ $ & An agentic operator comprising a set of LLM instances, a textual prompt, and a set of temperature settings.
$ M $ & An individual LLM instance.
$ M $ & The set of all feasible LLMs.
$ P $ & A textual prompt used as input to the LLM.
$ P $ & The feasible space of prompts.
$ T $ & The temperature setting of the LLM.
$ O $ & The set of all feasible agentic operators.
$ G = \ V , E \ $ & A multi-agent system represented as a graph with vertices $ V $ and edges $ E $.
$ A = \ , O \ = \ _ ( O )\ _ O O \ _ =1 ^L$ & An $L$-layer probabilistic agentic supernet, consisting of a distribution $ $ and a set of feasible operators $ O $.
$ $ & The distribution associated with the agentic supernet.
$U( G ; q,a)$ & The utility evaluator of $ G $ with respect to query $q$ and answer $a$.
$C( G ; q,a)$ & The cost evaluator of $ G $ with respect to query $q$ and answer $a$.
$ Q _ $ & The controller network parameterized by $ $.
$e(a\| G )$ & Execution of $ G $ to produce the answer $a$.
$ V _ $ & The selected operators at layer $ $ of the agentic supernet $ A $.
$ O _ exit $ & The early-exit operator.
$ v ( )$ & The text embedding function.
$ _ L $ & The gradient of the loss $ L $ with respect to the distribution $ $.
$ _ O L $ & The textual gradient of the loss $ L $ with respect to the operators $ O $.

1.2pt

In this section, we detail the initialization of operator nodes as follows:

Chain-of-Thought (CoT).
CoT cot reasoning encourages the LLM to think step by step rather than directly outputting an answer.
This approach enhances its capability to solve complex problems through intermediate reasoning steps, improving task handling and providing greater transparency in the decision-making process.
LLM-Debate.
LLM-Debate arXiv2023_MultiAgent-Debate allows multiple LLMs to debate, leveraging diverse perspectives to identify better solutions.
In practice, we initialize three debaters and permit up to two debate rounds.
Self-Consistency.
Adopting the methodology from wang2023selfconsistency , this operator aggregates five CoT reasoning paths and determines the final answer through majority voting.
Self-Refine.
Following NeurIPS2023_Self-Refine , this operator initially generates an answer using CoT reasoning, then prompts the agent to self-reflect iteratively.
We set a maximum of five refinement iterations.
Ensemble.
Inspired by LLM-Blender blender , this operator involves three LLM-powered agents from different sources outputting answers to the same query.
The pairwise ranking is used to evaluate and aggregate their responses into a final solution.
Testing.
Following the test designer in AgentCoder , this operator is used for generating test cases for the generated code.
ReAct.
Following yao2023react , this operator enables the agent to leverage versatile tools, including code interpreter, web searching, external knowledge database, etc.
, to handle diverse user demands.
Early exit.
We introduce the early exit operator, which interrupts the multi-agent architecture sampling process and enables the depth of the agentic supernet to be query-dependent.
We respectfully note that the selection of these operators is highly customizable, allowing users the flexibility to incorporate their desired operators into the operator repository of .
Following established practices , we first employ an LLM to generate a comprehensive profile description for each operator.
Subsequently, a lightweight text embedding model (in our case, MiniLM wang2020minilm ) is used to encode the profile into a fixed-dimensional embedding.

The prompt for generating the operator profile is as follows:

[notitle, sharp corners, breakable, colframe=Periwinkle, colback=white,
boxrule=3pt, boxsep=0.5pt, enhanced,
shadow= 3pt -3pt 0pt opacity=1,mygrey ,
title= Embedding Prompt ,]

pcr

prompt = """You are a highly proficient expert in designing and defining operators for large language models (LLMs).
Your primary objective is to meticulously generate the `description` and `interface` fields for a specified operator based on its provided Python implementation.
The generated content must be accurate, efficient, and precisely reflect the functionality of the operator's code.

To ensure consistency, quality, and adherence to best practices, refer to the following examples of previously defined operators:

"Generate":
"description": "Generates anything based on customized input and instruction.",
"interface": "generate(input: str, instruction: str) -> dict with key 'response' of type str"
,
"ScEnsemble":
"description": "Uses self-consistency to select the solution that appears most frequently in the solution list, improving the selection to enhance the choice of the best solution.",
"interface": "sc_ensemble(solutions: List[str], problem: str) -> dict with key 'response' of type str"

Now, given the following operator code.

This code encompasses the function signature, parameters with type annotations, internal logic, and return statements essential for comprehensively understanding the operator's purpose and behavior.Please provide its `description` and `interface` fields in the same format.

[operator code]

"""

The implementation of the textual gradient component is partially adapted from the repositories and .
We would like to explicitly acknowledge this contribution and express our sincere gratitude to the authors for their open-source efforts.

[notitle, sharp corners, breakable, colframe=Periwinkle, colback=white,
boxrule=3pt, boxsep=0.5pt, enhanced,
shadow= 3pt -3pt 0pt opacity=1,mygrey ,
title= Textual Gradient ,]

pcr

base = """
# Overview
You are an expert machine learning researcher specializing in designing agentic systems.
Your objective is to create building blocks such as prompts and control flows within these systems to solve complex tasks.
Specifically, you aim to design an optimal agent that performs exceptionally on the HumanEval benchmark.
The HumanEval dataset evaluates code generation capabilities in AI systems, consisting of 164 hand-crafted Python programming problems.
Generate a complete, syntactically correct Python function that strictly adheres to the given requirements.

Problem:
input

Follow these steps:
1.

Analyze the problem requirements and identify edge cases
2.

Design a solution that passes all implied test cases
3.

Implement the function with clear variable names and comments

Ensure:
- The code directly implements the requested functionality
- All parameters and return types match the problem specification
- Exception handling for edge cases is included when necessary '''

def __init__(self, llm: LLM, name: str = "Generate"):
super().__init__(llm, name)

async def __call__(self, input: str, mode: str = None):
prompt = self.GENERATE_PROMPT.format(input=input)
response = await self._fill_node(GenerateOp, prompt, mode="xml_fill")
return response

# Discovered architecture archive
Here is the archive of the discovered operator architectures:
[ARCHIVE]

# Output Instruction and Example:
The output should be a JSON object with the following structure.The first key should be ("thought"), and it should capture your thought process for designing the next operator.
The second key ("description") corresponds to the brief description of your next operator.
Finally, the last key ("code") corresponds to the exact operator and its prompt in Python code that you would like to try.
You must write COMPLETE CODE in "code": Your code will be part of the entire project, so please implement complete, reliable, reusable code snippets.
- thought: Captures your thought process for designing the next operator.
- Reason about what the next interesting operator should be.
- Describe your reasoning and the overall concept behind the operator design.
- Detail the implementation steps.
- description: A brief description of your next operator.
- code: The exact operator and its prompt in Python code.
Ensure the code is complete, reliable, and reusable.

Here is an example of the output format for the next operator:
[operator_example]

You must strictly follow the exact input/output interface used above.
Also, it could be helpful to set the LLM's role and temperature to further control the LLM's response.
DON'T try to use some function that doesn't exist.
In __call__(), you need to specify the instruction, input information, the prompt and the required output fields class for operators to do their specific part of the architecture.

# Your task
You are highly proficient in prompting techniques and well-versed with agentic systems from academic literature.
Your goal is to maximize performance metrics by proposing innovative and effective new operators.

Instructions:
1.
Analyze the Discovered Operators: Carefully review the operators in the archive to identify strengths, weaknesses, and areas for improvement.
Draw Insights: Extract lessons and insights from existing operators to inform the design of the next operator.
Innovate: Think creatively to design an operator that addresses current limitations or explores new functionalities, drawing inspiration from related agent papers or other research areas.
Design the Operator: Propose the next operator's `thought`, `description`, and `code` following the specified format.
Ensure Completeness: The generated code must be complete, reliable, and reusable, fitting seamlessly into the existing architecture.

Execution Steps:
1.
Insert Operator Code: Replace the `[ARCHIVE]` and `[operator_example]` placeholders with actual content as needed.
Generate Output: Produce the `thought`, `description`, and `code` fields for the new operator, ensuring adherence to the guidelines.
Validate Output: Ensure the generated JSON is correctly formatted and the code is syntactically and functionally correct.
THINK OUTSIDE THE BOX and leverage interdisciplinary insights to enhance the agentic system's capabilities.

"""

Building upon established methodologies in workflow automation , we divide each dataset into training and test sets using a train:test ratio of 1:4.

For the MATH benchmark, we adhere to , selecting a subset of 617 harder problems spanning four representative categories, Combinatorics \& Probability, Number Theory, Pre-algebra, and Pre-calculus, all at difficulty level 5.
The dataset statistics are included in tab:dataset .

In this section, we provide a detailed description of the configurations for baseline methods:

CoT.
Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting guides LLM agents to break down reasoning into sequential steps rather than generating direct answers.
We employ the implementation from .
ComplexCoT.
We follow the official implementation available at .
Self-consistency.
To enhance robustness, we aggregate five CoT-generated solutions.
LLM-Debate.
We instantiate five LLM-agents, each assigned a distinct role, which participate in up to two rounds of debate, after which the final decision is determined via majority voting.
The implementation is based on .
LLM-Blender.
We choose two gpt-4o-mini , one Qwen-2.5-72b , and one llama-3.1-70b to empower LLM-Blender .
DyLAN.
We directly utilize the implementation from .
AgentVerse.
The experimental setup follows the original implementation from .
MacNet.
For MacNet qian2024scaling , we adopt the ``MacNet-MESH'' variant, which corresponds to a fully connected network topology.
GPTSwarm.
The method is implemented in accordance with the original settings described in .
AutoAgents.
We adhere to the official configuration specified in .
ADAS.
The implementation details are directly inherited from .
AgentSquare.
We utilize the modular search framework introduced in .
The base LLM remains fixed at gpt-4o-mini , with early stopping set to a patience of 5 iterations.
AFlow.
In , AFlow operates with both gpt-4o-mini and claude-3.5-sonnet .
To maintain fairness under homogeneous conditions, we restrict AFlow to gpt-4o-mini and set max\_iteration =20.
We have visualized the evolution of operator sampling trends as the sampling count increases, in fig:distribution .
learns to avoid overly confident early stopping and instead prioritizes testing and self-refinement in deeper layers.
